epigraph.text: Gather the fragments left over, that nothing may be lost.
epigraph.quote: The integral is a limit of sums—but which sums depends on the path.

content.text:
We now define the integral of a complex function along a contour. The 
definition parallels line integrals from multivariable calculus but 
yields uniquely complex-analytic results.

content.title: Contour Integral
content.content:
Let $f$ be continuous on a contour $\gamma: [a, b] \to \C$. The 
**contour integral** of $f$ along $\gamma$ is
\[
 \int_\gamma f(z)\,dz = \int_a^b f(\gamma(t)) \gamma'(t)\,dt.
\]

content.text:
The right-hand side is an ordinary integral of a complex-valued function 
of a real variable, computed by integrating real and imaginary parts 
separately:
\[
 \int_a^b g(t)\,dt = \int_a^b \re(g(t))\,dt + i\int_a^b \im(g(t))\,dt.
\]

content.title: Integral around a circle
content.problem:
Compute $\displaystyle\int_{|z|=1} z^n\,dz$ for integer $n$, where 
$|z| = 1$ denotes the unit circle traversed counterclockwise.
content.solution:
Parametrize: $\gamma(t) = e^{it}$, $t \in [0, 2\pi]$, so 
$\gamma'(t) = ie^{it}$.

\[
 \int_{|z|=1} z^n\,dz = \int_0^{2\pi} e^{int} \cdot ie^{it}\,dt 
 = i\int_0^{2\pi} e^{i(n+1)t}\,dt.
\]

If $n \neq -1$:
\[
 i\int_0^{2\pi} e^{i(n+1)t}\,dt = i \cdot \frac{e^{i(n+1)t}}{i(n+1)}\bigg|_0^{2\pi} 
 = \frac{e^{2\pi i(n+1)} - 1}{n+1} = \frac{1 - 1}{n+1} = 0.
\]

If $n = -1$:
\[
 i\int_0^{2\pi} 1\,dt = 2\pi i.
\]

content.title: A remarkable result
content.content:
The integral $\oint_{|z|=1} \frac{dz}{z} = 2\pi i$ is fundamental. This 
single computation underlies much of complex analysis, including the 
residue theorem.

content.title: Properties
content.content:
Let $f, g$ be continuous on $\gamma$.

1. **Linearity:** $\displaystyle\int_\gamma (af + bg)\,dz 
 = a\int_\gamma f\,dz + b\int_\gamma g\,dz$

2. **Reversal:** $\displaystyle\int_{-\gamma} f\,dz 
 = -\int_\gamma f\,dz$

3. **Additivity:** $\displaystyle\int_{\gamma_1 + \gamma_2} f\,dz 
 = \int_{\gamma_1} f\,dz + \int_{\gamma_2} f\,dz$
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
If $f(z) = u(x,y) + iv(x,y)$ and $z = x + iy$, then $dz = dx + i\,dy$, so
\[
 \int_\gamma f\,dz = \int_\gamma (u + iv)(dx + i\,dy) 
 = \int_\gamma (u\,dx - v\,dy) + i\int_\gamma (v\,dx + u\,dy).
\]
This expresses the complex integral as two real line integrals.

marginNotes.text:
In all your ways acknowledge him, and he 
will make straight your paths.

marginNotes.author: Karl Weierstrass
marginNotes.text:
A mathematician who is not also something 
of a poet will never be a complete mathematician.
